institution_affiliations.notes: Served in Chilesso, Angola for 20 years. Established hospital staffed by indigenous Angolan people she trained.

notable.notable_blurb: First woman on Maine State Medical Board; Angola

narrative_blocks.header: First woman on the Maine State Medical Board
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Floyd Cushman earned her medical degree from Boston University School of Medicine in 1892, practiced medicine in Massachusetts and Maine for nearly 30 years, and was appointed the first woman member of the Maine State Medical Board. Then, in 1922 at the age of 52, she sailed for Angola as a medical missionary. She had cared for her elderly mother for 20 years before making the transition to missionary work.

In 1944 she published Missionary Doctor: The Story of Twenty Years in Africa (Harper & Brothers). The New York Times called her 'a person of apparent energy and equally apparent kindliness' who accomplished a thorough success. The book remains a source on Angolan Ocimbundu culture and colonial-era medical practice.

political_offices.title: Regent
political_offices.jurisdiction: University of the State of New York

notable.notable_blurb: U.S. Congressman, New York circuit judge, Litchfield Law

narrative_blocks.header: U.S. Congressman, circuit judge, and rector of Troy
narrative_blocks.detail: Cushman prepared for Yale at Plainfield Academy alongside John Adams (Yale 1795) and read law under Judge Zephaniah Swift of Windham before arriving at Litchfield. At Litchfield he became close friends with William L. Marcy — who went on to serve as U.S. Senator, Governor of New York, Secretary of War, and Secretary of State. Cushman's Litchfield Law School classmate John Allen Collier gave a memorial address at the opening of the Supreme Court after his death. He was also a trustee of Union College from 1833 until his death, and a regent of New York University. His father-in-law was Colonel Benjamin Tallmadge, Washington's spymaster. (Wikipedia; Litchfield Historical Society)
narrative_blocks.body: John Paine Cushman graduated Yale in 1807, studied at the Litchfield Law School in 1808, and was admitted to the bar in 1809. He was elected to the 15th Congress (1817-1819) as a Federalist, then served as Recorder of Troy (1834-1838) and Judge of the Third Circuit Court (1838-1844). Upon his death in 1848, the U.S. Supreme Court adjourned in his honor.

narrative_blocks.detail: The mansion remained in the family's orbit through much of the 19th century. Maria's father Colonel Benjamin Tallmadge was a frequent correspondent; their social circle included the leading legal and political figures of early New York State. (Landmark; YouTube documentary)
narrative_blocks.body: The Cushman family home at 39 First Street in Troy, built in 1820, is a four-story all-brick Federal-style house. Its original owners were direct descendants of Revolutionary War heroes and Plymouth Colony settlers.

institutions.institution_blurb: Attended 1808; admitted bar 1809; permanently settled Troy NY.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: His 1820 Troy townhouse, near the Washington Park he helped found.

notable.notable_blurb: Litchfield Female Academy; daughter of Culper Ring's Benjamin

narrative_blocks.header: Student at the Litchfield Female Academy
narrative_blocks.detail: Maxcy's friend Lemuel Williams wrote to him on Christmas Eve 1808, the week before his own wedding: 'O! my Virgil! How ardently I wish that we might all meet together, you & your Maria, I and my Sarah, in the bonds of wedded love.' The letter suggests Maria had already ended the engagement by then. Cushman and Maxcy had been classmates at Litchfield Law School — making the social circle of early American legal education small enough that broken hearts and new marriages overlapped by a season. (Galloway-Maxcy-Markoe Papers, Library of Congress; Litchfield Historical Society)
narrative_blocks.body: Maria Jones Tallmadge studied at the Litchfield Female Academy in 1801 and 1802. Before meeting John Cushman, she was engaged to Virgil Maxcy, a Litchfield Law School student. A surviving letter from Tapping Reeve consoling Maria suggests the break came around 1807 — after which John Cushman, who had just arrived at the same law school, became her husband in 1812.

narrative_blocks.detail: Benjamin Tallmadge is credited with uncovering the treason of Benedict Arnold and thwarting the British capture of West Point. He corresponded with his son-in-law John Cushman on legal matters throughout the 1820s. A 1824 letter survives in which Tallmadge wrote to Cushman about a domestic incident at his Litchfield home — a reminder that even the Culper Ring's spymaster kept up a lively family correspondence. (Litchfield Historical Society; Wikipedia on Benjamin Tallmadge)
narrative_blocks.body: Maria's father was Colonel Benjamin Tallmadge, Yale 1773, who ran General Washington's spy network — the Culper Ring — during the Revolutionary War. He later served as a U.S. Congressman. Her maternal grandfather was General William Floyd, member of the Continental Congress and signer of the Declaration of Independence.

cross_connections.display_label: is the mother of Edwards Williams, who married Mary Floyd Cushman

INST041.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM)
INST041.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM)
INST041.notes: Founded 1810 at Andover Seminary, Massachusetts by prominent Congregationalists. First American foreign missionary society; first missions to China and Ceylon. Yale President Rev. Timothy Dwight IV (H00914) was among the founding figures; Dwight Mission (established 1820 in present-day Arkansas/Oklahoma for the Cherokee) was named in his honor. The board operated Brainerd Mission (Chattanooga TN, est.1817) where Harriet Ellsworth Blunt (X02121) and Ainsworth Blunt (X02122) served the Cherokee. HOOKER TREE CONNECTIONS: 1. Rev. Timothy Dwight IV (H00914) — Yale President 1795-1817; founding ABCFM figure; Dwight Mission named for him. 5th-generation Hooker descendant. 2. Elias Cornelius (X00724) — ABCFM Secretary of Domestic Correspondence 1819-1832; conducted 1817 missionary tour to Creek and Cherokee; m. Mary Ann Hooker (H01355). 3. Rev. Edward William Hooker (H01354) — corporate ABCFM member from 1840. 4. Harriet Ellsworth Blunt (X02121) — served at Brainerd Mission 1821-1837; granddaughter of Rev. Cyprian Strong (H00299). 5. Ainsworth Blunt (X02122) — Brainerd missionary 1822-1837; accompanied Cherokee on Trail of Tears 1838; first mayor of Dalton GA. 6. Dr. Mary Floyd Cushman (HD2818, gen 8) — ABCFM medical missionary in Angola 1922-1953. 7. Samuel Gelston Dwight (HD2376) — ABCFM missionary to Hawaii; children named for ABCFM colleagues Juliette Cooke and Samuel Castle.
